subject: Internal medicine
title: Pelvic inflammatory disease
preambleMarkdown:
# Pelvic inflammatory disease

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pelvic inflammatory disease is a clinical condition or syndrome in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Pelvic inflammatory disease to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pelvic inflammatory disease is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Pelvic inflammatory disease depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Pelvic inflammatory disease.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Pelvic inflammatory disease disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Pelvic inflammatory disease should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Pelvic inflammatory disease first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Pelvic inflammatory disease, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Pelvic inflammatory disease, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Pelvic inflammatory disease presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Pelvic inflammatory disease with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Pelvic inflammatory disease is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Pelvic inflammatory disease should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Pelvic inflammatory disease is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Pelvic inflammatory disease, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Pelvic inflammatory disease may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Pelvic inflammatory disease patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Pelvic inflammatory disease, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Pelvic inflammatory disease in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Pelvic inflammatory disease, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Pelvic inflammatory disease does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Pelvic inflammatory disease may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Pelvic inflammatory disease, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Pelvic inflammatory disease as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Pelvic inflammatory disease draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Vaginitis, Cervicitis, and Pelvic Inflammatory Disease

_Source lines: 26200-26461._

```text
VI. VAGINITIS, CERVICITIS, AND PELVIC INFLAMMATORY DISEASE
A. Vaginitis
1. Discussion. Vaginitis is inflammation of the vulva and vaginal vault.

a. Predisposing factors include multiple unprotected sexual exposures, high
estrogen states (e.g., use of oral contraceptives, pregnancy), antibiotic use,
diabetes, and immunosuppression.

b. Etiology
i. Infection is the leading cause of vaginitis.

a) Candida albicans, a fungus, is not usually sexually transmitted.

b) Trichomonas vaginalis is a sexually transmitted, protozoal infection
involving the cervical and vaginal epithelium.

c) Gardnerella (Haemophilus) vaginalis, a bacterium, is not generally sex-
ually transmitted, although this infection is common in sexually active
women. Symptoms occur when there is overgrowth of normal flora in
the vaginal vault (e.g., in immunocompromised or diabetic patients).

ii. Foreign bodies (e.g., tampons, sanitary napkins) may produce vaginal
irritation and a malodorous discharge.

iii. Chemical irritation (e.g., from medications, douches, perfume) can
cause local irritation and acute tenderness during intercourse (dyspa-
reunia). Physical examination may reveal an erythematous, inflamed
vaginal vault without discharge or cervical motion tenderness.

iv. Reduced estrogen levels in postmenopausal women result in a more
friable vaginal mucosa, leading to atrophic vaginitis (characterized by
pruritus, erythema, and tenderness).

2. Clinical features. Inflammation of the vulva and vaginal vault leads to itchy,
tender, erythematous tissues and increased vaginal discharge. Patients may also
complain of dyspareunia or dysuria.

a. C. albicans vaginitis is characterized by a "cottage-cheese" discharge in the
cervix and in the vaginal vault. The patient may have mild cervical motion
tenderness but no uterine or adnexal tenderness.

b. Trichomoniasis. Patients complain of a thin, "frothy," malodorous dis-
charge, vaginal pruritus, dysuria, and dyspareunia. Examination reveals an
erythematous, tender vagina and cervix ("strawberry cervix").

c. Bacterial vaginitis. Patients complain of a thin, foul "fishy-smelling" dis-
charge, vaginal pruritus, and dyspareunia. Cervical motion tenderness may
be present.

3. Differential diagnoses include pinworm infection, acute cystitis, cervicitis, and
sexually transmitted infections, such as gonorrhea and chlamydia.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Patient history. It is important to obtain a complete gynecologic and men-
strual history.

b. Laboratory studies
i. Culture. Vaginal and cervical discharge should be cultured and evalu-
ated microscopically.

a) C. albicans vaginitis. A potassium hydroxide preparation, sodium
chloride preparation, or Gram staining may reveal yeast buds.

b) Trichomoniasis. A sodium chloride microscope slide preparation
reveals round, flagellated, motile protozoa.

Quick 	HIT
All women with abdominal or
pelvic pain, vaginal bleeding,
or discharge should be eval-
uated with a pelvic exam.

326 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Obstetric and Gynecologic Emergencies
c) Bacterial vaginitis. A sodium chloride preparation may reveal "clue"
cells (i.e., epithelial cells studded with bacteria).

ii. Syphilis serologies should be sent when a sexually transmitted agent is
suspect.

iii. Urinalysis (on a sample obtained by catheterization) and culture
should be performed to rule out concurrent urinary tract involvement.

## 5. Therapy

a. Infectious vaginitis
i. Fungal vaginitis
a) C. albicans vaginitis is treated with miconazole suppositories (200
mg each evening for 3 to 7 days), oral fluconazole (150 mg orally
once), or terconazole suppositories (80 mg each evening for 3 days).

b) Other fungal agents of vaginitis (e.g., Torulopsis glabrata) may be
resistant to conventional antifungal treatment and may need longer
therapy, repeated dosing, or systemic antifungal treatment.

ii. Trichomoniasis is treated with oral metronidazole (2 g administered in
a single dose). In pregnant patients, a 7-day course of metronidazole
(500 mg twice daily) or clotrimazole suppositories (inserted each eve-
ning for 7 days) can be used.

iii. Bacterial vaginitis is treated in similar fashion to trichomoniasis. Metroni-
dazole (500 mg twice daily for 7 days). Treatment with clotrimazole sup-
positories can be considered for pregnant patients with severe infections.

b. Foreign object vaginitis. Object removal and proper hygiene education.

c. Contact vaginitis is treated by terminating exposure to the offending agent.

d. Atrophic vaginitis is improved by applying topical estrogen cream daily for
5 to 10 days.

6. Disposition. Patients require short-term follow-up with a gynecologist after
evaluation in the ED.

B. Cervicitis
1. Discussion. Cervicitis involves inflammation of the cervix, producing dyspa-
reunia and cervical motion tenderness on examination. The following microor-
ganisms can cause cervicitis:
a. Neisseria gonorrhoeae is a gram-negative diplococcus that infects up to 90%
of patients after a single sexual encounter with an infected partner.

b. Chlamydia trachomatis, the most common sexually transmitted organism in
the United States, is an obligate, intracellular bacterium.

c. Herpes simplex virus is a DNA virus of two serotypes. Type 2 is predomi-
nantly associated with genital lesions, although type 1, more commonly
causing stomatitis, may be found in 20% of patients. Transmission is via
direct mucous membrane contact.

2. Clinical features
a. Gonococcal cervicitis. The incubation period is 2 to 5 days, although up
to 75% of patients with gonococcal cervicitis may be asymptomatic for
months. Patients with symptoms note abnormal vaginal discharge, lower
abdominal pain, and dyspareunia. Examination reveals a yellowish, muco-
purulent discharge from the cervix and tenderness on digital manipulation.

No adnexal tenderness is noted.

b. Nongonococcal cervicitis. The incubation period for Chlamydia is 6 to 21 days,
and 60% to 70% of patients may have an asymptomatic "latent" period. Pa-
tients with acute cervicitis may complain of an abnormal vaginal discharge.

Examination reveals an erythematous, friable cervix with motion tenderness.

c. Viral cervicitis. Most patients have lesions involving the vulvovaginal and
cervical region. The development of hyperesthetic tissues and red, papular
eruptions, which evolve into ulcerating vesicles that eventually crust and
heal, occurs after an incubation period of 3 to 7 days. Constitutional symp-
toms include malaise, fever, myalgias, and headache. Herpetic lesions are ex-
quisitely tender to touch, making pelvic examination difficult. Symptoms may
persist for 7 to 20 days with gradual resolution and also reactivation later.

O B S T E T R I C A N D G Y N E C O L O G I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	327

Obstetric and Gynecologic Emergencies
3. Differential diagnoses include bacterial or protozoal vaginosis, PID, and cervi-
cal cancer.

## 4. Evaluation

a. Gonococcal cervicitis. Gram stain of the discharge may show gram-negative
intracellular and extracellular diplococci.

b. Nongonococcal cervicitis. Gram stain of the discharge is rarely revealing.

Diagnosis is usually confirmed with a cervical culture.

c. Viral cervicitis. Tzanck smear of an ulcer crater may reveal multinucleated
giant cells. Viral polymerase chain reaction for herpes simplex virus DNA is
becoming test of choice in many laboratories.

5. Therapy. Due to high rates of coinfection, treatment for bacterial infection is
directed at gonococcal and chlamydial infection.

a. Gonococcal. Ceftriaxone (250 mg in one intramuscular dose) plus doxycy-
cline (100 mg orally every 12 hours for 7 days). Doxycycline and tetracy-
clines should be avoided in pregnant women.

b. Chlamydial. Azithromycin (1 g administered in a single oral dose) or a
7-day course of doxycycline.

c. Viral. Herpes virus becomes latent inside neuronal cells and is impossible
to eradicate from the body. Acyclovir (200 mg orally five times daily or
400 mg three times daily for 10 days) or valacyclovir (1 g orally twice daily
for 10 days) are used to limit initial and reactivation symptoms.

C. PID
1. Discussion. PID is an ascending infection (from the lower genital tract) that involves
acute infection of the uterus and fallopian tube. PID rarely occurs during pregnancy
because the cervical mucus plug prevents infection from traveling beyond the cervix.

a. Incidence. The incidence is 1 in 100 women of childbearing age (i.e., be-
tween the ages of 15 and 40).

b. Risk factors include unprotected intercourse, multiple partners, prior infection,
pelvic procedures (e.g., dilatation and curettage), and intrauterine device use.

c. Etiology. Over 75% of infections are sexually transmitted. Causative organ-
isms include C. trachomatis, N. gonorrhoeae, and Mycoplasma hominis. Nor-
mal vaginal flora may also ascend through the cervix, causing symptoms.

d. Complications include infertility (20% risk after first infection), ectopic
pregnancy, recurrent infection, and chronic pelvic pain. PID may also be
complicated by gonococcal perihepatitis (Fitz-Hugh-Curtis syndrome) or
the development of tubo-ovarian abscess.

2. Clinical features. PID is characterized by vague, lower abdominal pain with
diffuse cervical, uterine, and adnexal tenderness. A vaginal or cervical dis-
charge and a fever may also be noted.

3. Differential diagnoses include pyelonephritis, appendicitis, tubo-ovarian ab-
scess, ovarian cyst, ovarian torsion, endometritis, enteritis, inflammatory bowel
disease, cholecystitis, and diverticulitis.

4. Evaluation. Symptoms range from severe to subclinical (no overt symptoms):
lower abdominal tenderness, dyspareunia, fever, irregular bleeding, abnormal dis-
charge, cervical motion tenderness, and unilateral or bilateral adnexal tenderness.

## 5. Therapy

a. Outpatient therapy. Ceftriaxone (250 mg administered in a single, intra-
muscular injection) plus doxycycline and metronidazole for 14 days
b. Inpatient therapy. Appropriate regimens include:
i. A cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone, 1 g every 24 hours; cefotetan, 2 g
every 12 hours; or cefoxitin, 2 g every 6 hours) plus doxycycline (by
mouth or IV)
ii. Clindamycin (900 mg every 8 hours) plus gentamicin (an initial load-
ing dose of 2 mg/kg followed by a maintenance infusion of 1.5 mg/kg
every 8 hours intravenously)
iii. Ampicillin/sulbactam (3 g IV four times daily) plus doxycycline
6. Disposition. Admission criteria include severe, systemic toxicity; an inabil-
ity to tolerate oral antibiotic or pain medications; suspected or confirmed
Quick 	HIT
Over 50% of patients with
chlamydial cervical infection
will have concomitant gono-
coccal infection.

Quick 	HIT
Tubo-ovarian abscess, best
diagnosed by pelvic ultra-
sound, and Fitz-Hugh-Curtis,
a clinical diagnosis, often
require surgical intervention
and admission.

328 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Obstetric and Gynecologic Emergencies
tubo-ovarian abscess or Fitz-Hugh-Curtis Syndrome; failed outpatient treat-
ment; and concomitant pregnancy. Inpatient therapy should also be considered
for patients who have no history of PID and for infected nulliparous patients
who desire pregnancy at a later date.
```

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.
